items.q: How does the graph of y = f(x - 3) compare to y = f(x)?
items.correct: It is translated 3 units to the right.
items.misconception: It is translated 3 units to the left because of the minus.
items.random_wrong: It is reflected in the y-axis because of the minus.
items.why: Students apply the sign literally; the minus 'pushes left' intuitively, but x - 3 actually requires x to be larger to get the same output, shifting the graph right.

items.misconception: 15, because 15% means 15 units out of any amount.
items.why: Students treat 'X percent of' as if X is the answer directly, not as a proportion to apply to the amount.

items.correct: -9 — without brackets, the exponent applies only to the 3, then the negative is applied: -(3^2) = -9.
items.misconception: 9, because squaring a negative number always gives a positive.
items.why: Students don't distinguish -3^2 from (-3)^2; the first is -9 because squaring binds tighter than the unary minus, but the second is +9.
The answer examiners want is: reject H₀. “accept H₀” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: rejecting H₀ when it is true
items.misconception: accepting H₀ when it is false
items.why: It's rejecting H₀ when it is true. “accepting H₀ when it is false” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: At α = 5%, what is the critical p-value threshold (give as a decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
